sections.title: Part 1: Multiple Matching (Reading)
sections.instructions: Answer questions 1-12 by referring to the newspaper article about sportsmen on page 35.
sections.passageHtml:
THEY THINK IT'S ALL OVER

In sport, the fall from stardom can be hard and sudden. Some come to a tragic end, others drift happily into mundane jobs.

A. Stan Bowles

Retirement was never a problem for Stan Bowles. When he chatted to Cliff Morgan about his life and career, Morgan asked him how much he missed the adulation, the glory, the limelight of professional sport. Had he felt lonely? “Well,” said Bowles. “I know it does take a lot of players like that. But you have to remember I had my betting to fall back on.” Morgan was silent.

B. Rodney Marsh

One of Bowles’s team-mates in 1970s football, Rodney Marsh, was outwardly prepared for the transition — more successful, an intelligent man with a chance to do a host of things outside or inside the game — but he wasn’t as happy-go-lucky as Bowles. He tells of driving his car down the Cromwell Road after he had packed in the game. He got to a red light. “I just thought ‘Why not?’ and put my foot down. I was hoping something was coming the other way and would crash into me. I just had no idea what I was going to do.”

C. Bob Latchford

The problem is worse, though, for those whose life has been played out in a team from 16 to 35. “The trouble is that you never imagine yourself not as a player,” says Bob Latchford, Everton and England football centre-forward of the 1970s. “You may never see the possibility that one day you won't be part of it. Even if you’ve thought about it, being dropped into the real world still comes as a shock. Mentally, it’s very hard to cope with the fact that you’ve got to do something else for the next 30-odd years.” Latchford struggled for a while. He ran a childrenswear business. Then he worked for a company that runs betting shops and then got back into the game as a youth coach at Birmingham City Football Club.

D. Frank Bruno

The problems sportsmen face once they have to get to grips with real life after a career as part of a team are very real. Some even go as far as killing themselves. The anger in Frank Bruno’s eyes as he increasingly lost his temper at home and which forced his wife to seek a court order against him is, say some sports psychologists, part of the same problem - the frustration of not being important, of not being the focus, of having to get to grips with the mundane reality of normal life. “When I came out of boxing, it was like a bereavement,” says Bruno. “Every morning, I had got out of bed at 6am to train, even on Christmas Day, and now I was getting up with nothing to do. My trainer once warned me that the toughest fight I would ever have would be out of the ring adjusting to life, and he was right. I had so many confused thoughts, I didn’t know which way to turn. I went into self-destruct.”

E. Brendan Batson

For the modern footballer, though, things are much better. Since 1984, the Professional Footballers Association has run an education scheme to prepare players for the outside world. “For sportsmen, the problem is that they are finishing a career at 35,” says Brendan Batson of the PFA, “they are often having to start at the bottom rung of other professional ladders where their contemporaries are often 15 years ahead of them. Often they have to take a pay cut, too, for which they aren’t prepared.” Batson’s own career was ended at 31 by injury. “That is hard,” he says, “because you don’t have time to adjust.”

F. John Watson

The problem for many sports people is that they never quite get around to planning for the day when the floodlights go out. But in sport, the fall can be hard and sudden. John Watson was one of Britain’s leading Formula One drivers until 1983 when he was suddenly dropped by McLaren in favour of Alain Prost. “You try to put it off, you try not to think about it, though you know it can happen,” says Watson. “And it hurts when suddenly you are not part of it all any more. When I went back to a Grand Prix in Belgium in 1984, I walked into the paddock and felt like a stranger, like I just didn’t belong here any more even after ten years in the game. I came away in a not very good emotional state.” Watson was lucky, he drove for Jaguar in sportscar racing and is now a successful broadcaster.

questionGroups.instruction: For questions 1-12, answer by choosing from the list of people (A-F). Some of the choices may be required more than once.

questions.explanation: Chọn B — Rodney Marsh. Đoạn văn thuật lại việc ông lái xe xuống đường Cromwell Road và cố tình vượt đèn đỏ: “I just thought ‘Why not?’ and put my foot down. I was hoping something was coming the other way and would crash into me.” (Tôi chỉ nghĩ 'Tại sao không?' và nhấn ga. Tôi đã hy vọng có thứ gì đó đang đi từ hướng ngược lại đâm vào tôi). Đây là một hành động cực kỳ nguy hiểm, biểu hiện của sự tự hủy hoại bản thân. ⚠️ Bẫy: Thí sinh dễ nhầm với Frank Bruno (D) vì ông này có xu hướng bạo lực trong gia đình (“increasingly lost his temper at home”), hoặc Stan Bowles (A) do lối sống cá cược, nhưng hành vi lái xe liều mạng muốn tự sát của Marsh mới là “a very dangerous incident” cụ thể. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.

questions.instruction: 2. helps other sportsmen adjust to the ending of their careers?
questions.explanation: Chọn E — Brendan Batson. Batson làm việc cho Hiệp hội Cầu thủ Chuyên nghiệp (PFA), tổ chức vận hành chương trình giáo dục từ năm 1984 nhằm chuẩn bị cho các cầu thủ đối mặt với thế giới bên ngoài: “Since 1984, the Professional Footballers Association has run an education scheme to prepare players for the outside world. ‘For sportsmen, the problem is...’ says Brendan Batson of the PFA.” ⚠️ Bẫy: Tránh chọn nhầm các vận động viên khác vì họ chỉ tự điều chỉnh cuộc sống của riêng họ hoặc chia sẻ khó khăn cá nhân chứ không trực tiếp vận hành một chương trình hỗ trợ diện rộng như Batson. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.

questions.instruction: 3. seems to have experienced the least difficulty?
questions.explanation: Chọn A — Stan Bowles. Khi được hỏi về việc giải nghệ, Bowles coi đó hoàn toàn không phải vấn đề vì ông có thói quen cá cược để khỏa lấp thời gian: “Retirement was never a problem for Stan Bowles... ‘I know it does take a lot of players like that. But you have to remember I had my betting to fall back on.’” ⚠️ Bẫy: Rodney Marsh (B) tỏ ra ngoài đời rất sẵn sàng (“outwardly prepared”) nhưng thực chất bên trong lại rất bất ổn và không hề vô tư như Bowles (“but he wasn’t as happy-go-lucky as Bowles”). Đừng để bị lừa bởi vẻ bề ngoài của Marsh. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.

questions.instruction: 4. said something that left an interviewer speechless?
questions.explanation: Chọn A — Stan Bowles. Khi Bowles thật thà chia sẻ rằng ông không cô đơn khi giải nghệ vì đã có việc cá cược làm điểm tựa tinh thần (“I had my betting to fall back on”), người phỏng vấn Cliff Morgan đã hoàn toàn im lặng: “Morgan was silent.” ⚠️ Bẫy: Nhiều thí sinh nhầm lẫn với Rodney Marsh vì Marsh có câu thoại rất sốc về việc muốn tự tử bằng ô tô, nhưng người nghe câu chuyện đó không được mô tả là im lặng hay mất lời ngay lúc đó như Cliff Morgan phỏng vấn Bowles. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.

questions.instruction: 5. appeared to be someone who would cope well with the end of his sports career?
questions.explanation: Chọn B — Rodney Marsh. Đoạn văn nêu rõ Marsh có vẻ ngoài rất sẵn sàng cho việc chuyển giao sự nghiệp, thông minh và có nhiều cơ hội làm việc khác: “Rodney Marsh, was outwardly prepared for the transition — more successful, an intelligent man with a chance to do a host of things outside or inside the game...” ⚠️ Bẫy: Đây là bẫy từ đồng nghĩa: “appeared to be” tương đương với “outwardly” (bề ngoài có vẻ). Thực chất Marsh lại rất đau khổ, nhưng bề ngoài ông lại tạo cảm giác sẽ đương đầu tốt. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. questions.explanation: Chọn D — Frank Bruno. Do không thể thích nghi với cuộc sống tẻ nhạt sau khi giải nghệ, Frank Bruno thường xuyên nổi giận ở nhà đến mức vợ ông phải xin lệnh cấm từ tòa án chống lại ông: “lost his temper at home and which forced his wife to seek a court order against him”. ⚠️ Bẫy: Cần phân biệt rõ giữa khó khăn trong gia đình (domestic) với khó khăn về tâm lý cá nhân hoặc tài chính của các vận động viên khác. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. questions.instruction: 7. returned to his sport after trying other things?
questions.explanation: Chọn C — Bob Latchford. Sau khi giải nghệ bóng đá, ông đã thử điều hành một doanh nghiệp quần áo trẻ em, rồi làm việc cho một công ty cá cược trước khi quay lại với bóng đá làm huấn luyện viên đội trẻ: “He ran a childrenswear business... and then got back into the game as a youth coach at Birmingham City Football Club.” ⚠️ Bẫy: John Watson (F) cũng chuyển nghề nhưng ông làm bình luận viên truyền hình (broadcaster) chứ không quay lại trực tiếp tham gia môn thể thao F1 như Latchford quay lại làm huấn luyện viên bóng đá. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.

questions.instruction: 8. felt angry at no longer being a star?
questions.explanation: Chọn D — Frank Bruno. Sự bức dọc của ông bắt nguồn từ việc không còn được là tiêu điểm chú ý, không còn quan trọng nữa: “the frustration of not being important, of not being the focus, of having to get to grips with the mundane reality of normal life.” ⚠️ Bẫy: Nhiều người nhầm với John Watson (F) vì ông cảm thấy như một người xa lạ khi quay lại trường đua, nhưng cảm xúc của Watson là buồn bã cô đơn (“felt like a stranger”) chứ không phải tức giận và mất kiểm soát (“lost his temper”, “anger”) như Bruno. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: D. Frank Bruno. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.

questions.instruction: 9. felt a sense of being an outsider?
questions.explanation: Chọn F — John Watson. Khi quay lại trường đua Grand Prix ở Bỉ vào năm 1984 sau khi giải nghệ, ông cảm giác mình như một người xa lạ và không còn thuộc về nơi này nữa: “I walked into the paddock and felt like a stranger, like I just didn’t belong here any more even after ten years in the game.” ⚠️ Bẫy: Cần chú ý cụm từ khóa “felt like a stranger” (cảm thấy như một người lạ) và “didn’t belong here” (không thuộc về nơi này) phản ánh trực tiếp trạng thái cảm giác là “outsider” (người ngoài cuộc). Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.

questions.instruction: 10. says that ex-sportsmen may have to start new jobs at the lowest level?
questions.explanation: Chọn E — Brendan Batson. Ông chỉ ra rằng các vận động viên giải nghệ thường phải bắt đầu lại từ nấc thang thấp nhất của các ngành nghề khác: “they are often having to start at the bottom rung of other professional ladders where their contemporaries are often 15 years ahead of them.” ⚠️ Bẫy: Tránh nhầm với Bob Latchford (C) mặc dù ông này cũng bắt đầu làm kinh doanh nhỏ, nhưng người đưa ra nhận định tổng quan về “bottom rung of ladders” (nấc thang dưới cùng) là Batson đại diện cho tổ chức PFA. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.

questions.instruction: 11. experienced a sudden and unexpected end to his career?
questions.explanation: Chọn E — Brendan Batson. Sự nghiệp của ông kết thúc đột ngột ở tuổi 31 do chấn thương: “Batson’s own career was ended at 31 by injury. ‘That is hard,’ he says, ‘because you don’t have time to adjust.’” ⚠️ Bẫy: John Watson (F) cũng bị thay thế đột ngột bởi Alain Prost (“suddenly dropped by McLaren”), nhưng ông đã có sự chuẩn bị tâm lý nhất định và không bị chấm dứt hoàn toàn hoạt động đua xe do chấn thương thể xác đột ngột làm mất khả năng vận động như Batson. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.

questions.instruction: 12. received accurate advice from his coach?
questions.explanation: Chọn D — Frank Bruno. Huấn luyện viên của ông từng cảnh báo rằng cuộc đấu khó khăn nhất của ông chính là việc điều chỉnh cuộc sống sau khi bước ra khỏi võ đài, và Bruno thừa nhận điều này hoàn toàn đúng: “My trainer once warned me that the toughest fight I would ever have would be out of the ring adjusting to life, and he was right.” ⚠️ Bẫy: Tránh nhầm với John Watson (F) vì ông cũng gặp khó khăn khi rời đội McLaren, nhưng không có lời khuyên hay cảnh báo cụ thể nào từ huấn luyện viên (trainer/coach) được nhắc tới. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. sections.title: Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)
sections.instructions: For questions 13-18, choose which of paragraphs A-G fits into the numbered gaps in the following newspaper article. There is one extra paragraph which does not fit in any of the gaps.
sections.passageHtml:
Davis, near Sacramento, in California, has a population of forty thousand. It also has forty thousand bicycles and just nine thousand cars. Cyclists have right of way everywhere in the town, seventy kilometres of bike lanes all to themselves, and their own unit of specially trained cycle police. The American obsession with the motor car would seem to have been tamed, at least in this one small corner of the USA.

[ Gap 13 ]

As a university town, Davis has always had more than its fair share of bicycles — and of ecological awareness too. It is home to the University of California’s Faculty of Agriculture, and back in the early seventies, a group of students and local people got together to draw up a comprehensive environmental and social plan of action to steer Davis away from the terminal urban sprawl that afflicts so many other American cities.

[ Gap 14 ]

Energy saving is at the top of this local agenda, and by the year 2000, the people of Davis expect the Californian sun to be providing half of all their energy needs. In pursuit of this objective, the town council has introduced many regulations which are designed to keep energy use to the absolute minimum.

[ Gap 15 ]

This is a very quaint custom by American standards! The majority of people have taken to sorting out their household waste into different piles, separating paper, metal and plastic, for example, so that specially designed dust carts can take it away for recycling.

[ Gap 16 ]

The size of building plots has been drastically reduced, so housing densities are high and there is enough room left over to make cheap allotments available to all flat owners without a garden.

[ Gap 17 ]

There are popular street markets twice a week where locally grown produce is bought and sold, giving everyone a chance to enjoy fresh food and to plough profits straight back into neighbouring farms.

Two long-term residents of the town, Jan and Jim Hogan, told an interesting story of how, when their daughter was in her teens, she could hardly wait to head out of Davis and make a life for herself in the big city. But now she is back in the town where she grew up, with her husband and small son in tow, because she could not think of a better place to raise her own family.

[ Gap 18 ]

Given such a track record, it should not come as a surprise that the vast majority of people in Davis support the changes that have been made.

questions.explanation: Chọn D. Đoạn văn trước khoảng trống giới thiệu về việc có 40.000 xe đạp so với 9.000 ô tô tại Davis. Đoạn D tiếp tục so sánh không gian chiếm dụng của xe đạp và ô tô: “The fact that one hundred and fifty bicycles take up the same space as twenty cars means that they have been busily turning car-parks into green oases...” (Thực tế là 150 xe đạp chiếm diện tích tương đương 20 xe ô tô có nghĩa là họ đã bận rộn biến các bãi đậu xe thành ốc đảo xanh). ⚠️ Bẫy: Tránh nhầm với đoạn C nói về khái niệm môi trường chung chung, vì đoạn D liên kết trực tiếp bằng số liệu cụ thể về xe đạp/ô tô đã nêu ngay trước khoảng trống. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 36 Method: match by paraphrase and discourse logic, then eliminate partial matches. Trap: a repeated keyword alone is not enough; check reference, contrast and the full claim.
questions.answerLocation: Davis, near Sacramento, in California, has a population of forty thousand.
options: A. Planning by-laws in Davis are based on the premise that a ‘green city’ is not a contradiction in terms. Houses are restricted to two storeys and business premises to four.

B. This kind of personal story is not surprising: Davis has no slums, no ghettos, very little unemployment and the lowest crime rate in the United States. People leave their doors unlocked. It actually works!

C. It is we ‘townies’ who are the ones with the most to gain by putting the notion of ‘the environment’ firmly at the centre of our lives — in terms of the air we breathe, the water we drink, the quality of our food and the conviviality of our communities.
D. The fact that one hundred and fifty bicycles take up the same space as twenty cars means that they have been busily turning car-parks into green oases rather than the other way around.

E. At forty square metres, one of these allotments can provide seventy per cent of all fruit and veg for a two-person household if properly managed. Organically managed, that is — for Davis is pretty much a pesticide-free town.

F. Three of the students got themselves elected to the city council and initiated a flow of legislation that has affected every aspect of local life since then. One of those students, Bill Carter, is the current mayor of the town.

G. Strict building standards are enforced and thousands of trees have been planted to discourage people from installing air-conditioning. The trees provide shade exactly where it is most needed. People in Davis even hang out their washing to dry instead of throwing it in the tumble dryer.
questions.source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Pearson Longman, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.explanation: Chọn F. Đoạn văn trước khoảng trống đề cập đến: “a group of students and local people got together to draw up a comprehensive environmental and social plan...”. Đoạn F tiếp nối thông tin về nhóm sinh viên này: “Three of the students got themselves elected to the city council and initiated a flow of legislation...” (Ba trong số các sinh viên đã trúng cử vào hội đồng thành phố và bắt đầu ban hành hàng loạt luật lệ...). ⚠️ Bẫy: Nhiều thí sinh chọn đoạn A vì nghĩ đến các quy định quy hoạch đô thị (“Planning by-laws”), nhưng đoạn F mới liên kết trực tiếp danh từ “students” với câu trước đó. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 36 Method: match by paraphrase and discourse logic, then eliminate partial matches. Trap: a repeated keyword alone is not enough; check reference, contrast and the full claim.

E. At forty square metres, one of these allotments can provide seventy per cent of all fruit and veg for a two-person household if properly managed. Organically managed, that is — for Davis is pretty much a pesticide-free town.

questions.explanation: Chọn G. sections.title: Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)
sections.instructions: Read the following article from a journal and then answer questions 19-24.
sections.passageHtml:
‘The whale has no voice’, wrote Melville in Moby Dick ‘but then again what has the whale to say? Seldom have I known any profound being that had anything to say to this world, unless to stammer out something by way of getting a living.’ Not so. Whales may not sing for their suppers, but some of them certainly do sing. Melville failed to hear them because they sing underwater. Others have heard them without realising it. If whales sing near a wooden-bottomed boat, sailors in their bunks or hammocks may hear an eerie melodious wail from they know not where. Hence, perhaps, the many sea-tales of lullabies sung by drowned colleagues.

Why do they sing? First, spot the singers. There are two sorts of whales: the toothed whales - such as sperm, killer and pilot whales — who are close relatives of the porpoise and the dolphin; and the toothless ‘baleen’ whales - such as the humpback, right and minke. The toothed whales usually live in stable and organised groups: a gang of killer whales may stay together for years on end. Such creatures make sounds, but have not been known to sing. Many dolphins produce ‘signature’ whistles - each one has a different call-sign from his neighbours. These seem to function as names: a dolphin will often produce his neighbour's whistle when nearby. Similarly, each sperm whale produces a distinctive series of clicks — known as his coda — and will sometimes mimic a nearby whale’s coda. Killer whales have identifiable dialects that are specific to each family.

It is the baleen whales, especially the humpbacks, who break into song. At any one time, all the singing whales in a population sing the same song. It gradually changes over time and each whale learns and copies the new variations. This is a formidable feat because the songs, which can last up to thirty minutes, are highly complex. It is only the males who sing, and they do so chiefly during the breeding season. The songs seem — like many bird songs — to be a sort of display that males use in competing with each other for females.

Singing humpbacks have a wide vocal range: the notes can swoop down from a high-pitched factory whistle to a reverberating fog-horn. Play back a recorded humpback song at fourteen times the correct speed and it sounds like a nightingale. But birdsong is shorter — and more significantly — not so structured as whale song. Whale song can be broken down into regularly repeating phrases, which in turn are organised into themes that always occur in the same sequence. Unlike birds, whales appear to have studied some of the rules of classical composition.

By analysing these themes and phrases, two scientists have reached conclusions about whale culture that would have struck Melville dumb. Whales seem to use a structure like rhyme in poetry. And, like people, they may put in the rhymes to help them remember their songs. The two scientists, Miss Linda Guinee of the Long Term Research Institute in Lincoln, Massachusetts, and Mrs Katharine Payne of Cornell University in Ithaca, New York, analysed 460 whale songs from the North Pacific and 88 from the North Atlantic. From their recordings they produced audio spectograms, converting sounds into strings of squiggles which can be classified by shape. Having a catalogue of whale songs helps marine biologists to track whale populations on their odysseys by showing where each singer comes from. It also lets them study the songs as they evolve. Since whales learn their songs, such songs are an example of culture; and the way they change is an example of cultural evolution.

It turns out that whales make much use of phrases with the same endings - i.e. rhymes. Miss Guinee and Mrs Payne found that songs with many differing themes were much more likely to contain plenty of rhymes. They found that rhymes were correlated not with the length of a song but with the amount of different material to be remembered. Simple songs did not contain any rhyming passages. The rhyming pattern then, could be a way to help the whale remember what comes next in a complicated song. A rhyming pattern helps people to learn and remember poetry by limiting the number of possible words in a given position. If you know that every third and fourth line of a stanza in a given poem rhyme, and that the third line of a stanza ends with ‘love’, you know that the next line might end with ‘dove’ but cannot end with ‘sparrow’. Advertising jingles often use rhyme in the justified hope that people will remember the names of products. You do not have to know the meaning of a word or sign in order for rhyme to help you recall it (think of children’s nonsense rhymes). Poets use rhyme for all sorts of reasons: because they are attractive, musical, create a pleasing rhythm, or are merely ingenious or funny.

Miss Guinee and Mrs Payne realise that their evidence is inconclusive, because they cannot ask the whales what they are up to. Some will doubt their conclusion because it makes whales seem implausibly human. Maybe they have not made whales ‘human’ enough. Perhaps the rhyming sirens of the deep are simply trying to please themselves and their audience.

questions.instruction: 19. What did Melville believe about whales?
questions.explanation: Chọn A — They didn’t produce vocal sounds. Melville viết: “The whale has no voice” (Cá voi không có giọng nói). Điều này phản ánh niềm tin của ông là cá voi không tạo ra âm thanh tiếng nói/vocal sounds. ⚠️ Bẫy: Tránh chọn B (“They had nothing to say”) vì Melville viết mang tính ẩn dụ “what has the whale to say?”, nhưng luận điểm chính thức của ông về mặt sinh học vẫn là “has no voice” (không có giọng nói - tức là không tạo âm thanh). Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 38 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: A. They didn’t produce vocal sounds. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. They didn’t produce vocal sounds.
B. They had nothing to say.
C. They couldn’t imitate the human voice.
questions.source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Pearson Longman, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 20. Which of the following seem to use personal identification signals?
questions.explanation: Chọn C — sperm whales. Trong văn bản, tác giả viết: “each sperm whale produces a distinctive series of clicks — known as his coda — and will sometimes mimic a nearby whale’s coda.” (mỗi con cá voi nhà táng tạo ra một chuỗi tiếng click đặc trưng - được gọi là coda của nó). Coda này đóng vai trò như tín hiệu nhận dạng cá nhân giống như tiếng huýt sáo của cá heo. ⚠️ Bẫy: Tránh nhầm với killer whales (cá voi sát thủ) vì chúng có “identifiable dialects that are specific to each family” (tiếng địa phương theo từng gia đình) chứ không phải tín hiệu nhận dạng cá nhân (personal identification) cho từng con đơn lẻ. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 38 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: C. sperm whales. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
questions.source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Pearson Longman, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 21. What is known about the song of a humpback whale?
questions.explanation: Chọn D — It is rather more complex than that of birds. Văn bản ghi nhận: “But birdsong is shorter — and more significantly — not so structured as whale song.” (Nhưng tiếng chim hót ngắn hơn và quan trọng hơn là không có tính cấu trúc bằng bài hát của cá voi). Điều này có nghĩa bài hát cá voi phức tạp và có cấu trúc chặt chẽ hơn nhiều so với chim. ⚠️ Bẫy: B sai vì nếu tua nhanh 14 lần thì nghe giống chim hót chứ không phải nếu làm chậm lại (“if it was slower”). options: A. It is identical to that of a nightingale.
B. It could be taken for birdsong if it was slower.

questions.instruction: 22. Why does the writer say ‘Whales appear to have studied some of the rules of classical composition’?
questions.explanation: Chọn D — He is trying to amuse his readers. Nhận định này mang tính hài hước, nhân hóa cá voi học luật soạn nhạc cổ điển để làm cho bài viết sinh động và giải trí hơn đối với người đọc. ⚠️ Bẫy: Tránh chọn A vì đây chỉ là cách viết lối văn phong chứ không phải một kết luận khoa học nghiêm túc rằng cá voi thực sự đi nghiên cứu âm nhạc cổ điển. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 38 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. options: A. He believes whales are more intelligent than most people think.
B. He doesn’t take the research seriously.
C. He thinks there is evidence that whales have learnt through contact with humans.
D. He is trying to amuse his readers.

questions.instruction: 23. What did the two scientists discover?
questions.explanation: Chọn D — The songs demonstrated where the whales originated from. Đoạn văn nêu: “Having a catalogue of whale songs helps marine biologists to track whale populations on their odysseys by showing where each singer comes from.” (Có một danh mục các bài hát cá voi giúp các nhà sinh học đại dương theo dõi quần thể cá voi trong các cuộc du ngoạn bằng cách chỉ ra mỗi ca sĩ đến từ đâu). ⚠️ Bẫy: A sai vì 460 và 88 là số lượng bài hát được phân tích chứ không phải số lượng “các loại bài hát” (types of song) hiện có. B. Whale song varied according to how fast the whales were travelling.
C. Whales responded when music was played to them.
D. The songs demonstrated where the whales originated from.

questions.instruction: 24. Where are rhymes most likely to occur?
questions.explanation: Chọn B — in complicated songs. Văn bản chỉ ra: “Miss Guinee and Mrs Payne found that songs with many differing themes were much more likely to contain plenty of rhymes... Simple songs did not contain any rhyming passages.” (phát hiện ra các bài hát có nhiều chủ đề khác nhau - tức là bài hát phức tạp - có khả năng chứa nhiều vần điệu hơn... Những bài hát đơn giản không chứa bất kỳ đoạn vần điệu nào). ⚠️ Bẫy: Nhiều thí sinh nhầm với “in long songs” (D), nhưng nghiên cứu đã nhấn mạnh vần điệu tương quan với lượng nội dung khác nhau cần ghi nhớ (amount of different material) chứ không phải độ dài thời gian của bài hát: “rhymes were correlated not with the length of a song but with the amount of different material”. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 38 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. sections.title: Part 4: Multiple Matching (Reading)
sections.instructions: Answer questions 25-42 by referring to the information writers give about themselves on pages 41-42.
sections.passageHtml:
Writers Do It Lying Down!

How and where do writers write? With coffee and cacophony or in austere silence? In warm kitchens or in lonely attics? Joanna Trollope follows the muse of successful novelists.

Rose Tremain

I've got the study I’ve always longed for. It looks out onto a big sloping lawn, and as soon as I go into it in the morning, it just takes me in. I like to start the day slowly, giving external attention to breakfast but some internal attention to the writing day ahead. I'm at my desk by ten. I like to be alone then and I like silence. I hate winter. My study has to be at least 70 degrees or I can’t concentrate. I write in longhand with Berol Mirado pencils on recycled paper. I'm aware of the need to be fit and well so I eat conscientiously at lunchtime. I stop at 5.30, do exactly 35 minutes of yoga, put on some Mozart or Haydn, and play around with food, which is a wonderful way of engaging other senses than the ones I use all day.

Patrick Gale

One of the joys of being a writer is that you don’t have to have a routine, but when I’ve got a book on the boil I really don’t do anything else. My brain only works until lunchtime and food is vital to keep me going: digestive biscuits are a must and I do confess to the odd chocolate crisis. I work until I’m really hungry, and then I take the dog out to thrash out any problems. After lunch, I'll read — mostly novels by dead authors so as not to get too demoralised. If it's warm, I work in a summerhouse I built, which has a view of the garden. If it's very cold, I shut myself into the loft. I have to confess I work better there. It’s where the word processor lives, although I like to work with pencil and exercise book first, to tease ideas out. The evenings are for the box. I love it. I video old films. That’s my treat.

Jilly Cooper

When I’m on a book, I'll work all day, every day. I start about ten, and I'll go on and on, until eight or nine. There's nothing in the middle except a dog walk and a chat to the horses. There’s not even any food really, because I’m always trying to lose weight. The room where I work is serious chaos. The room faces south, so my typist Monica and I trek about to whichever surface has no sun and the least mess. I keep longhand notebooks of all the events and characters in the current book and a file for each chapter. If Leo's here, it's great, because he'll usually cook supper. By then I’m only fit to slump in front of the telly.

Julian Barnes

When I get to my desk at about ten, I find I don't very much want to be there. Luckily, this feeling passes. My desk is in a light, upstairs room painted Chinese yellow. There are two prunus trees outside, which bullfinches seem to like, and once I saw a jay. I'm fairly easily distracted, and will roam off to get mugs of coffee, biscuits and raisins, and wait hopefully by the letter box for the postman. The best creative time is from ten to one. I work on a big black electric typewriter. I don't want a word processor. When I’m working on a novel, I'll put in a seven-day week. Cooking’s helpful if I’m stuck. I don’t really reward myself at the end of a good day. I'm just relieved to feel less guilty.

Susan Hill

When I was single, I had a lovely long morning that began at about eight, with nobody to think about but me. There's no doubt it's harder now, having got up at 6.30 and done the school run, and having one’s head seething with domestic things. You have to guard against that dangerous, restless time when you return home and can get waylaid so easily. I'm working in a Portakabin in the garden while we convert a barn into my study. I take coffee in there and sit quietly, thinking myself down into the book again. I have little breaks for more coffee, but this doesn’t interfere with concentration at all, nor does my stunning view. I stop at lunchtime and that's it really. I use jumbo economy pads and write in longhand. I prefer pencil. I used to type the manuscript myself but then I found a wonderful lady who can read my writing. I’m useless with machines and their noise would drown the words in my head.

Sir Fitzroy Maclean

I wrote Eastern Approaches 45 years ago, and I've had a book on the go ever since. I travel all the time and I get on with writing wherever I am, buses, helicopters, airports, anywhere. In 1946, in America, I bought a portable typewriter but when I'd typed 150,000 words on it, I thought, ‘Never again.’ I like yellow spiral-backed pads and those floating ball pens. My ideal is to write in the library, or in my specially insulated room at home in Scotland, or at the kitchen table in London, sustaining myself with a huge pot of China tea. If I’m travelling, I take a flask for tea — it's vital. I like regular meals and I'm inclined to sleep after lunch. My book is my first thought every day, and it's my escape from real life. I don’t need a reward at the end of a day. The writing is a prize in itself.

sections.passageHtml:
That Sweet Smell of Success is ...

There is a revolution in the retail world that cannot (0) fail to attract shoppers’ noses. In the latest marketing ploy, smells are created in laboratories to be wafted around stores in order to (43) ..... the unsuspecting into spending more money. Secret (44) ..... of the ‘designer’ smells are going on in more than a hundred stores across Britain, including bookshops, petrol stations and a (45) ..... of clothes shops. The tailor-made aromas (46) ..... coconut oil in travel agents (to (47) ..... exotic holidays), and leather in car showrooms (to suggest (48) ..... quality). Marketing Aromatics, a company specialising in this area, believes that odours are under-used as a marketing (49) ..... . Until now the most frequent (50) ..... has been in supermarkets where the smell from in-store bakeries has been blown among the (51) ..... to boost sales of fresh food. ‘We are taking things one stage further,’ said David Fellowes, the company’s commercial director. ‘We can build on customer loyalty by making customers (52) ..... a particular smell with a particular store. It is not intrusive. If it were it would defeat the (53) ..... .’

The smells are designed to work on three levels: to relax shoppers by using natural smells such as peppermint; to bring back memories using odours such as a whiff of sea breeze; and to encourage customer loyalty by using a corporate perfume ‘logo’ to (54) ..... a company’s image. Dr George Dodd, scientific adviser to Marketing Aromatics, believes smells can affect people’s moods. ‘It is a very exciting time. Smells have enormous (55) ..... to influence behaviour,’ he said. Critics say retailers are (56) ..... to subliminal advertising. ‘Not telling consumers that this is happening is an (57) ..... invasion of their privacy. People have the right to know,’ said Conor Foley of Liberty, the civil liberties association.

sections.instructions: For questions 58-72, complete the following article by writing each missing word. Use only one word for each space. The exercise begins with an example (0).
sections.passageHtml:
Volcanoes: Dangers and Benefits

There are fifteen capital cities in the world in a position to be wiped out (0) or seriously damaged by volcanic eruptions. So why then (58) .......... people continue to live in (59) .......... dangerous areas? Many of these people are poor and have (60) .......... choice, while others disregard the risk, (61) .......... is, after all, rather (62) .......... than the risks involved in smoking or driving a car. What attracts people to volcanic areas (63) .......... fertile land. The soils from volcanic ashes are light, easily worked, drain well and are full (64) .......... plant nutrients. A light fall of ash, (65) .......... it may destroy one year’s crop, often pays the farmer (66) .......... in future years (67) .......... the fertility it adds to the soil. Coffee in Colombia, vines in Italy and rice in Japan are (68) .......... a few of the crops that flourish on volcanic soils.

In Italy, Japan, New Zealand, the United States and Iceland the subterranean heat from volcanoes (69) .......... used in geothermal power stations to generate electricity. In many places, the way in which the flows of lava concentrate minerals (70) .......... them attractive to mining companies. There is growing evidence that gold is (71) .......... the minerals collected by volcanic flows, and the diamond-mining industry in South Africa takes advantage of the huge pressures within past volcanoes (72) .......... have produced diamonds.

Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 46 Method: complete the gap with "DO" only after checking the words before and after it. sections.passageHtml:
Notice: In Case of Fire

General Instructions

In an emergency, the first (0) responsibility [RESPONSIBLE] of all members of staff is to prevent injury or (89) ..... [LOSE] of life. If you discover a fire, operate the nearest alarm. The (90) ..... [SECURE] officer or his (91) ..... [AUTHORITY] deputy is responsible for contacting the Fire Brigade.

Evacuation Procedures

The (92) ..... [WARN] signal is a series of short pulses of the alarm. When this signal sounds:

  • switch off all machines
  • switch off all (93) ..... [ELECTRIC] and gas supplies
  • shut windows
  • evacuate physically (94) ..... [ABLE] staff

Evacuation Signal

This is a continuous sounding of the alarm. On hearing this signal, everyone should leave by the nearest fire exit.

  • walk, don't run
  • do not stop to collect (95) ..... [BELONG]
  • do not re-enter the building

Advertisement: Subscribe Now!

Over a year, a (96) ..... [SUBSCRIBE] to our magazine will save you £21 compared with making a monthly purchase at the newsagent's.

There are several methods of (97) ..... [PAY] — cheque, credit card or direct debit. Our (98) ..... [ADMINISTRATE] costs are much lower if you pay by direct debit, so there is a (99) ..... [REDUCE] of £2 in the price you pay — a total (100) ..... [SAVE] of £23 over the year. (Please note that this extra discount is not applicable if you pay the special student rate). If you choose this method, you don’t have to worry about (101) ..... [NEW] because your bank will (102) ..... [AUTOMATIC] transfer the money once a year. We will send you a (103) ..... [REMIND] a few weeks before it is time to renew. You can cancel at any time and will receive a full refund on any unposted copies.

sections.title: Part 5: Register Transfer (Use of English)
sections.instructions: For questions 104-116, read the Historical Cottages Trust Booking Terms leaflet and use the information to complete the numbered gaps in the informal letter. Use no more than two words for each gap. The words you need do not occur in the leaflet.
sections.passageHtml:
Historical Cottages Trust Booking Terms

  1. You are required to pay the total price three months before your holiday begins.
  2. Payments must be in sterling (internationally recognised credit cards are acceptable).
  3. If you cancel within three months of the start of your holiday, you will forfeit the booking fee (unless severe weather prevents you from reaching the property).
  4. You must not enter the property before 4 p.m. on the first day and you must not stay longer than 11 a.m. on the last day of your holiday. This is to enable our housekeeper to prepare the property for you and your successors.
  5. Dogs are permitted but must be kept off the furniture.
  6. Nobody except the people whose names are listed on the booking form must stay in the property. We mind VERY MUCH INDEED about this.
  7. Although our properties are in isolated areas, all are easily accessible by car.
  8. The cottages are fully equipped but sheets and towels are not provided.

Informal Letter

Dear Sophie,

I'm enclosing some information about an organisation that rents out interesting old cottages. We stayed in one last year and it was marvellous.

One disadvantage is that you have to pay (0) in full (104) ..... . Also, they only accept English (105) ..... but you can use a credit card. Don’t book unless you are sure because if you cancel they will not (106) ..... your money, unless you cancel more than three months before you intend to go, or unless the road to your chosen cottage has become (107) ..... because of bad weather.

Remember that you have to (108) ..... after 4 p.m. on the first day and (109) ..... before 11 a.m. on the last day so that the housekeeper can get the cottage ready for you, and the people who (110) ..... . It will be (111) ..... for you to take your dogs but don’t let them (112) ..... on the furniture. Remember that the (113) ..... people who may sleep in the cottage are those whose names are on the booking form. The Trust takes this matter (114) ..... .

You can (115) ..... to all the properties, so you can take with you whatever you need. You will have to take (116) ..... sheets and towels in any case.

Love,

Jenny

sections.instructions: For questions 117-122, read through the text below and then choose from the list A-J below the best phrase to fill each of the spaces.
sections.passageHtml:
A Telephone Pioneer: Henry Hunnings (1843-86)

Most people have heard of Alexander Graham Bell, who invented the telephone in 1876. But it was a little-known English priest called Henry Hunnings (0) who made the first big improvement. Bell’s model could transmit a voice up to about 23km. A year after its invention, Thomas Edison improved on the model by attaching a solid-carbon button inside the mouthpiece. But Hunnings decided to experiment with granular carbon (117) ..... . This improved the voice clarity and extended the voice-transmission distance to about 72km. Hunnings was granted a patent for his invention in December 1878.

Hunnings was born in 1843 near London. He became a priest in Yorkshire and although much of his time was taken up with Church matters, he had a profound interest in all sorts of gadgets, (118) ..... . A friend of Hunnings with similar interests, called Cox Walker, built a telephone receiver. In 1880 an American called Anders visited England and asked Walker (119) ..... . He was impressed and offered Hunnings £1,000 for the patent. The offer was accepted and the Globe Company was formed (120) ..... . In 1881 Hunnings was granted two patents in the United States for his invention. But by this time so many people and companies were involved in telephone design and manufacture (121) ..... . There was a famous court case in 1882 when the United Telephone Company took the Globe Company to court for an alleged infringement of its patents. At first it failed to have the Hunnings patent declared invalid (122) ..... . It then bought the rights to the entire patent from Hunnings.

questions.explanation: Chọn B — instead of solid carbon. Văn học nói về việc Hunnings quyết định thử nghiệm với cacbon dạng hạt (granular carbon) 'instead of solid carbon' (thay vì cacbon dạng rắn). Điều này tạo ra độ tương phản rõ rệt với 'granular carbon'. Source: CAE Practice Tests Plus 1, Test 2, Page 50 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar.
